excerpt: The designation of the Imām after the Prophet is by explicit text (naṣṣ), not by communal choice; obedience to the waṣī is the continuation of obedience to the Prophet.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-walāya, opening section on the obligation of recognising the Imām

excerpt: The verse 'O Messenger, deliver what has been sent down to you' was revealed at Ghadīr Khumm regarding the wilāya of ʿAlī, and its severity establishes the rank of the matter announced there.
source_location_hint: Section on the taʾwīl of the obligation of walāya

excerpt: At Ghadīr Khumm the Prophet established the walāya of ʿAlī by the formula 'whoever's master I am, ʿAlī is his master', and invoked friendship and enmity in correspondence with friendship and enmity towards ʿAlī.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-walāya, narration of the event of Ghadīr

excerpt: The Commander of the Faithful declared: 'Ask me before you lose me, for with me is the knowledge of the first and the last,' affirming his mastery over the sciences of revelation and taʾwīl.
source_authority: Compilation of the discourses of Imām ʿAlī
source_location_hint: Khuṭab — sermon on the comprehensiveness of his knowledge

excerpt: ʿAlī forbore from claiming his right by the sword lest the religion be torn asunder, preserving the unity of the community over the assertion of his own due.
source_authority: Compilation of the discourses of Imām ʿAlī
source_location_hint: al-Khuṭba al-Shiqshiqiyya

excerpt: The recognition (maʿrifa) of the Imāms is the highest rank of faith, and through them the believer is conducted to the inward truths of the religion.
source_location_hint: Introductory chapters on the rank of the Imāms and the duty of maʿrifa

excerpt: The Prophet and the waṣī are two lights from one divine light, manifest in human form so that the people may receive guidance through their visible bearers.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the origination of the lights of prophecy and walāya

excerpt: The Originator, Most High, is not apprehended directly; He is known to His servants through the ḥudūd of His daʿwa, who are His witnesses and the manifestations of His command.
source_location_hint: Mashraʿ on the ḥudūd and the manner in which Mubdiʿ Taʿālā is known

excerpt: Take account of yourselves before you are called to account, and weigh your deeds before they are weighed for you.
source_location_hint: Hikam (Short Sayings); cf. also Letter 31 to Imam al-Hasan on self-examination

excerpt: The story of Yusuf is read as a cycle of the da'wa: the prophet's beauty, the trial of Zulaykha, and her ultimate recognition figure the soul's passage from the bondage of the lower nature to the discipline of walaya under the rightful guide.
source_location_hint: Section on the cycle of Yusuf among the prophetic narratives

excerpt: The walaya of 'Ali is the foundation of the religion: by it deeds are accepted, by its denial they are nullified, for he is the gate of the Prophet's city of knowledge and the proof of God upon His servants after His Messenger.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya (Book I), opening chapters on the imamate of 'Ali and the obligation of walaya

excerpt: The Muhammadan Light and the Walawi Light are the two principles from which the ranks of the angels and the spiritual hierarchies derive their existence; every grace that descends to creation passes through them, and every act of obedience that ascends is presented through them.
source_location_hint: Majalis on the cosmic status of the Prophet and 'Ali and the angels' knowledge of them

excerpt: the inner sense of ṣalāt is the recognition of the walāya of the Imām, and the inner sense of zakāt is acknowledgement of his rank
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Ṣalāt and Kitāb al-Zakāt — bāṭinī exegesis of the pillars

excerpt: The Messenger of God said: 'I and ʿAlī are from one light' (anā wa-ʿAlī min nūr wāḥid)
source_location_hint: Vol. 1, Kitāb al-Walāya — narrations on the unitary light of the Prophet and ʿAlī

excerpt: And the likeness of the Garden which the God-fearing have been promised: in it are rivers of water unstaling, rivers of milk unchanging in flavour, rivers of wine — a delight to the drinkers — and rivers of honey purified.
source_location_hint: chapter on Paradise and its rivers; bāṭinī reading of the four streams as the four currents of knowledge from the Imāms

excerpt: nubuwwa and walāya as two faces of a single light: the Messenger announces the outward summons (ẓāhir), and the Friend of God carries its inward authority (bāṭin)
source_location_hint: discourses on the rank of the waṣī alongside the nāṭiq

excerpt: walāya is the first and chief of the seven pillars; obedience to the Messenger is fulfilled only through obedience to the one whom the Messenger appointed; this single chain is the firm handhold
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, opening sections on the precedence of walāya

excerpt: al-marʾu maʿa man aḥabba — a man is with whom he loves
source_author: al-Sharīf al-Raḍī (compiler); sayings of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_authority: compilation of the words of the Commander of the Faithful
source_location_hint: short sayings (ḥikam) section, on love and companionship

excerpt: God's forbearance (ḥilm) is the patience by which the seed of faith is permitted to ripen in successive generations; chastisement is held in abeyance until the appointed line is established
source_location_hint: sections on divine mercy, forbearance, and the unfolding of decree

excerpt: maḥabba (love) for the Imāms of guidance is itself an act of allegiance that places the lover within their saved company
source_location_hint: discourses on the merits of love for the Imāms and the Prophetic saying al-marʾu maʿa man aḥabba

excerpt: the rope of Q. 3:103 (wa-iʿtaṣimū bi-ḥabli-llāhi jamīʿan) read as the continuous cord of walāya stretching from the Prophet through the Imāms, by which the believer ascends from the world of generation to the world of light
source_authority: early Fatimid-era Ismaili dāʿī and son of the Yemeni dāʿī Ibn Ḥawshab
source_location_hint: taʾwīl of 'the firm handhold' and the chain of walāya

excerpt: maʿrifa (recognition) and ṭāʿa (obedience) are inseparable: to know the Imām is to be bound to act upon his command
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, traditions on the duty of recognition of the Imām

excerpt: walāya is the first of the seven pillars and the one without which the other four — prayer, alms, fast, and pilgrimage — cannot ascend, for it is walāya that opens the door to their acceptance
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, on the precedence of walāya over the remaining pillars

excerpt: Love of the imams without obedience to God is a claim that consumes itself; the friend of the Family is recognised by the discipline of his limbs, not by the protest of his tongue.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, on the necessity of conjoining maḥabba with ṭāʿa

excerpt: The walāya of the imams is the inner sense (bāṭin) of every act of worship the sharīʿa prescribes; without it the outward form is a body without a soul.
source_location_hint: Opening chapters on the taʾwīl of walāya and ṭahāra

excerpt: What the revelation calls prayer, alms, fasting, and pilgrimage points, in its inner reading, to the persons of the imams; and what it calls wine, idol, and carrion points to their adversaries — the Book is one tableau of friend and foe.
source_location_hint: Prolegomenon on the bipartite symbolism of the Qurʾān

excerpt: The Prophet's character was itself a proof, weightier in the scale of evidence than any sign worked upon stone or water; for in him the human form had been brought to the perfection for which it was first created.
source_location_hint: Majālis on the khuluq ʿaẓīm of the Prophet

excerpt: Silence in the Messenger was not absence of speech but the discipline of a tongue that would utter only what its Lord set upon it; his stillness was itself a form of revelation.
source_location_hint: Discourse on the comportment (adab) of the Prophet

excerpt: The lights of the Family were the first emanation, and from their tasbīḥ the angels learned to glorify; thus the celestial ranks themselves stand within the ambit of the prophetic light.
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the precedence of the muḥammadī light over the angelic orders

excerpt: The prostration commanded to the angels was directed to God through Ādam, but its inner cause was the light deposited within him — the trust (amāna) of the Family for whose sake the angels bowed.
source_location_hint: Chapter on the prostration of the angels to Ādam and its taʾwīl
